1.1: King David was old—advanced in years—and though they piled blankets on him, he could not get warm.
1.2: His servants said: 'Let a young woman be found for my lord the king—a virgin who can attend him and care for him. She will lie close to you, my lord, and keep you warm.'
1.3: So they searched all Israel for a beautiful young woman, and they found Abishag from Shunem and brought her to the king.
1.4: Abishag was very beautiful. She attended the king and served him, but the king did not have relations with her.
1.5: Meanwhile Adonijah son of Haggith was exalting himself, declaring, 'I will be king.' He assembled chariots and horsemen and fifty men to run ahead of him—just as Absalom had done.
1.6: His father had never once rebuked him or asked, 'Why do you do this?' He was also a very handsome man, and he was born after Absalom—the next in line by age.
1.7: He enlisted Joab son of Zeruiah and Abiathar the priest, and they threw their support behind him.
1.8: But Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, the prophet Nathan, Shimei, Rei, and David's elite warriors did not join Adonijah's cause.
1.9: Adonijah held a sacrificial feast at the Serpent's Stone beside En-rogel—sheep, oxen, and fattened cattle—and invited all his royal brothers and all the officials of Judah,
1.10: but he did not invite the prophet Nathan, or Benaiah, or the elite warriors, or his brother Solomon.
1.11: Nathan went to Bathsheba, Solomon's mother, and said: 'Have you not heard? Adonijah son of Haggith has declared himself king—and David our lord doesn't know it.'
1.12: Come, let me advise you—your life and Solomon's life depend on it.
1.13: Go in to King David and say: 'My lord the king, did you not swear to me by the LORD that Solomon my son would reign after you and sit on your throne? Then why is Adonijah king?'
1.14: While you are still speaking with the king, I will come in and confirm everything you have said.
1.15: Bathsheba went into the king's chamber. He was very old, and Abishag the Shunammite was attending him.
1.16: Bathsheba bowed to the ground before the king. 'What do you wish?' the king asked.
1.17: She said: 'My lord, you swore to your servant by the LORD your God: Solomon your son will reign after you and sit on your throne.'
1.18: And now, Adonijah has made himself king—and you, my lord, do not even know it.
1.19: He has slaughtered oxen, fattened cattle, and sheep by the score, and has invited all the king's sons, Abiathar the priest, and Joab the army commander—but Solomon your servant he did not invite.
1.20: My lord the king, all Israel is watching you, waiting to hear from your own lips who will sit on your throne after you.
1.21: When my lord the king dies and lies with his fathers, Solomon and I will be treated as traitors.
1.22: While she was still speaking, the prophet Nathan arrived.
1.23: They announced: 'Nathan the prophet is here.' He came in and prostrated himself before the king with his face to the ground.
1.24: Nathan said: 'My lord the king, have you commanded that Adonijah shall reign after you and sit on your throne?'
1.25: Today he went down and offered a great feast—oxen, fattened cattle, and sheep in abundance—and invited all the king's sons, the military commanders, and Abiathar the priest. Right now they are eating and drinking before him and shouting, 'Long live King Adonijah!'
1.26: But me—your servant—and Zadok the priest, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and your servant Solomon: he invited none of us.
1.27: Is this what my lord the king has authorized, without telling your servants who will sit on the throne after you?
1.28: King David said: 'Bring Bathsheba to me.' She came in and stood before the king.
1.29: The king swore: 'As the LORD lives—the one who has rescued my life from every danger—'
1.30: 'just as I swore to you by the LORD the God of Israel—Solomon your son will reign after me, and he will sit on my throne in my place—this I will carry out today.'
1.31: Bathsheba bowed to the ground in homage and said: 'May my lord King David live forever!'
1.32: King David said: 'Summon Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada.' They came before the king.
1.33: The king said: 'Take the royal guard with you. Have my son Solomon ride on my own mule, and bring him down to Gihon.'
1.34: 'There let Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anoint him king over Israel. Blow the trumpet and shout, Long live King Solomon!'
1.35: 'Come up behind him, and let him come and sit on my throne—he will reign in my place. I have appointed him ruler over Israel and Judah.'
1.36: Benaiah son of Jehoiada answered the king: 'Amen! May the LORD, the God of my lord the king, make it so!'
1.37: 'As the LORD has been with my lord the king, so may he be with Solomon—and may he make Solomon's throne even greater than yours, my lord King David.'
1.38: Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and the Cherethite and Pelethite guard went down. They set Solomon on King David's own mule and brought him to Gihon.
1.39: There Zadok the priest took the horn of oil from the tent and anointed Solomon. The trumpet sounded, and all the people shouted: 'Long live King Solomon!'
1.40: All the people went up after him, playing pipes and rejoicing with such great joy that the earth itself shook with the sound.
1.41: Adonijah and all his guests heard the noise as they were finishing their meal. Joab heard the trumpet and said: 'What is all this commotion in the city?'
1.42: While he was still speaking, Jonathan son of Abiathar the priest arrived. Adonijah said: 'Come in—you are a good man; you must be bringing good news.'
1.43: Jonathan answered: 'Not good news—at all. Our lord King David has made Solomon king.'
1.44: 'The king sent Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and the royal guard with him. They put Solomon on the king's own mule,'
1.45: 'and Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anointed him king at Gihon. They went up from there rejoicing—and that is the noise filling the city right now.'
1.46: Solomon is already seated on the royal throne.
1.47: 'And the king's own servants came and blessed David, saying: May God make Solomon's name more renowned than yours, and his throne greater than yours. And the king bowed in worship right there on his bed.'
1.48: 'The king also said: Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who has given me a son to sit on my throne this very day—and let me live to see it.'
1.49: All of Adonijah's guests were seized with terror. They got up and scattered, each going his own way.
1.50: Adonijah was terrified of Solomon. He got up and ran and seized the horns of the altar.
1.51: Word came to Solomon: 'Adonijah is clinging to the horns of the altar in fear. He is asking you to swear that you will not kill your servant with the sword.'
1.52: Solomon said: 'If he conducts himself well, not a hair on his head will be harmed. But if evil is found in him, he will die.'
1.53: King Solomon sent for him, and they brought him down from the altar. He came and bowed before King Solomon, and Solomon said: 'Go home.'

2.1: As David's death drew near, he charged his son Solomon:
2.2: 'I am going the way of all the earth. Be strong. Be a man.'
2.3: 'Keep the charge of the LORD your God: walk in his ways, keep his statutes, his commandments, his ordinances, and his testimonies, as written in the Law of Moses—so that you may prosper in everything you do and wherever you go.'
2.4: 'Then the LORD will keep the word he spoke about me—that if your descendants watch their path and walk before him in whole-hearted faithfulness, the dynasty will never lack a man on Israel's throne.'
2.5: 'You know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me—what he did to the two commanders of Israel's armies, Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether. He murdered both of them. In peacetime he shed blood as if it were war, and that war-guilt stained his belt and his sandals.'
2.6: 'Deal with him as your wisdom directs—but do not let that gray head go peacefully to the grave.'
2.7: 'Show covenant loyalty to the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite—let them eat at your table. They came to me with that same loyalty when I was fleeing from your brother Absalom.'
2.8: 'You also have Shimei son of Gera the Benjaminite from Bahurim, who cursed me bitterly on the day I fled to Mahanaim. When he came down to meet me at the Jordan, I swore by the LORD: I will not kill you with the sword.'
2.9: 'But do not let him go unpunished. You are a wise man—you know what to do. Bring his gray head down to the grave with blood.'
2.10: Then David rested with his fathers and was buried in the city of David.
2.11: David reigned over Israel forty years in all—seven in Hebron, thirty-three in Jerusalem.
2.12: Solomon took his seat on the throne of his father David. His kingdom was firmly established.
2.13: Then Adonijah son of Haggith came to Bathsheba, Solomon's mother. 'Do you come in peace?' she asked. 'In peace,' he said.
2.14: He said: 'I have something to say to you.' She said: 'Speak.'
2.15: He said: 'You know the kingdom was mine—all Israel expected me to reign. But the kingdom has passed to my brother. It was the LORD's to give, and he gave it to him.'
2.16: 'Now I have just one request—do not refuse me.' She said: 'Speak.'
2.17: He said: 'Please ask King Solomon—he will not refuse you—to give me Abishag the Shunammite as my wife.'
2.18: Bathsheba said: 'Very well. I will speak to the king on your behalf.'
2.19: Bathsheba went to King Solomon to speak on behalf of Adonijah. The king rose to meet her and bowed, then sat on his throne. A seat was placed for the queen mother, and she sat at his right hand.
2.20: She said: 'I have just one small request to make—do not refuse me.' The king said: 'Ask it, my mother; I will not refuse you.'
2.21: She said: 'Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to your brother Adonijah as his wife.'
2.22: King Solomon said to his mother: 'Why do you ask Abishag for Adonijah? You might as well ask me to give him the kingdom. He is my elder brother. He has Abiathar the priest and Joab son of Zeruiah on his side.'
2.23: King Solomon swore by the LORD: 'May God deal with me in the harshest terms if this request does not cost Adonijah his life!'
2.24: 'As the LORD lives—who established me and seated me on the throne of my father David, who built me a dynasty as he promised—Adonijah will die today.'
2.25: King Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada, who struck Adonijah down. He died.
2.26: To Abiathar the priest the king said: 'Go to Anathoth, to your own fields. You deserve to die—but I will not execute you now, because you carried the ark of the Lord GOD before my father David, and because you shared in all his suffering.'
2.27: So Solomon removed Abiathar from the priesthood. This fulfilled the word the LORD had spoken against the house of Eli at Shiloh.
2.28: The news reached Joab—he had sided with Adonijah, though not with Absalom—and Joab fled to the tent of the LORD and seized the horns of the altar.
2.29: Word came to King Solomon: 'Joab has fled to the tent of the LORD and is clinging to the altar.' Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada: 'Go, strike him down.'
2.30: Benaiah went to the tent of the LORD. 'The king says: Come out,' he said. But Joab said: 'No. I will die here.' Benaiah brought the king's reply back: 'This is what Joab said, and this is how he answered me.'
2.31: The king said: 'Do as he says—strike him down and bury him. Remove from me and from my father's house the blood Joab shed without cause.'
2.32: 'The LORD will return his bloodshed on his own head. He struck down two men more righteous than himself—without my father David knowing it. Abner son of Ner, commander of Israel's army, and Amasa son of Jether, commander of Judah's army.'
2.33: 'Their blood will fall on Joab's head and on his descendants forever. But on David, his descendants, his house, and his throne—peace from the LORD, now and forever.'
2.34: Benaiah son of Jehoiada went up and struck Joab down. He was buried at his own house in the wilderness.
2.35: The king appointed Benaiah son of Jehoiada over the army in Joab's place, and Zadok the priest in Abiathar's place.
2.36: The king sent for Shimei and said: 'Build a house in Jerusalem and live there. Do not leave for any reason, anywhere.'
2.37: 'The day you leave and cross the Kidron, know for certain: you will die. Your blood will be on your own head.'
2.38: Shimei said: 'What you say is good. Your servant will do exactly as my lord the king commands.' Shimei lived in Jerusalem for many years.
2.39: But three years later, two of Shimei's servants ran away to Achish son of Maacah, king of Gath. Shimei was told: 'Your servants are in Gath.'
2.40: Shimei saddled his donkey and went to Gath to Achish to reclaim his servants. He went and brought them back.
2.41: Solomon was told that Shimei had gone to Gath and returned.
2.42: The king sent for Shimei and said: 'Did I not make you swear by the LORD and give you fair warning? The day you leave Jerusalem and go anywhere, you will die. You said: What you say is good; I will obey.'
2.43: 'Then why did you not keep your oath to the LORD and the command I laid on you?'
2.44: The king said: 'You know in your own heart all the harm you did to my father David. The LORD is bringing that harm back on your own head.'
2.45: 'But King Solomon will be blessed, and the throne of David will stand before the LORD forever.'
2.46: The king commanded Benaiah son of Jehoiada, who went out and struck Shimei down. He died. And the kingdom was fully established in Solomon's hand.

3.1: Solomon made a marriage alliance with Pharaoh king of Egypt—he took Pharaoh's daughter and settled her in the city of David until he had finished building his own palace, the house of the LORD, and the wall around Jerusalem.
3.2: The people were still worshiping at the hilltop shrines—there was as yet no house built for the name of the LORD.
3.3: Solomon loved the LORD and walked in the statutes of his father David—though he still offered sacrifices and burned incense at the high places.
3.4: The king went to Gibeon to sacrifice—it was the greatest of the high places—and Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings there on the altar.
3.5: At Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream at night. God said: 'Ask. What shall I give you?'
3.6: Solomon said: 'You showed such faithful love to your servant David my father, because he walked before you in faithfulness, righteousness, and sincerity of heart. And you have continued that faithful love—you have given him a son sitting on his throne this very day.'
3.7: 'And now, LORD my God, you have made me king in my father David's place—though I am only a youth. I do not know how to lead or govern.'
3.8: 'Your servant stands among your chosen people—a great people, so vast they cannot be counted.'
3.9: 'Give your servant therefore a heart that truly listens—to govern your people, to tell right from wrong. For who can govern this great people of yours?'
3.10: The Lord was pleased that Solomon had asked for this.
3.11: God said to him: 'Because you asked for this—not long life, not wealth, not the death of your enemies—but for the discernment to do justice,'
3.12: 'I am doing as you asked. I am giving you a wise and discerning mind—there has been no one like you before, and there will be no one like you after.'
3.13: 'I also give you what you did not ask: wealth and honor, so that no king in all your days will compare to you.'
3.14: 'And if you walk in my ways—keeping my statutes and commandments as your father David did—I will give you long life.'
3.15: Solomon woke—it was a dream. He went to Jerusalem and stood before the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and gave a feast for all his servants.
3.16: Then two women who were prostitutes came to the king and stood before him.
3.17: The first woman said: 'My lord, this woman and I live in the same house. I gave birth while she was with me in the house.'
3.18: 'Three days after I gave birth, she also gave birth. We were alone together—no one else was there. Just the two of us.'
3.19: 'This woman's son died in the night—she lay on him.'
3.20: 'She got up at midnight while I slept, took my son from beside me, laid him at her breast—and put her dead son at mine.'
3.21: 'When I got up in the morning to nurse my child—he was dead. But when I looked at him in the morning light, I saw he was not the child I had borne.'
3.22: The other woman said: 'No—the living child is mine and the dead one is yours.' The first said: 'No—the dead one is yours and the living one is mine.' They argued like this before the king.
3.23: The king said: 'One says: This living child is mine and the dead child is yours. The other says: No—the dead child is yours and the living one is mine.'
3.24: The king said: 'Bring me a sword.' They brought a sword before the king.
3.25: The king said: 'Cut the living child in two. Give half to one and half to the other.'
3.26: The woman whose child was alive—her heart was torn open for her son—cried: 'My lord! Give her the living child! Do not kill him!' But the other said: 'Let him be neither mine nor yours. Divide him.'
3.27: The king gave his verdict: 'Give the living child to the first woman. Do not kill him. She is his mother.'
3.28: All Israel heard of the king's judgment, and they stood in awe of him—because they saw that the wisdom of God was in him to do justice.

4.1: King Solomon ruled over all Israel.
4.2: These were his chief officials: Azariah son of Zadok the priest;
4.3: Elihoreph and Ahijah sons of Shisha were secretaries; Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the recorder;
4.4: Benaiah son of Jehoiada commanded the army; Zadok and Abiathar served as priests;
4.5: Azariah son of Nathan oversaw the district governors; Zabud son of Nathan was a priest and the king's trusted friend;
4.6: Ahishar managed the royal household; Adoniram son of Abda oversaw the forced labor.
4.7: Solomon had twelve district governors over all Israel who supplied provisions for the king and his household. Each was responsible for one month of the year.
4.8: Their names: Ben-hur, in the hill country of Ephraim;
4.9: Ben-deker, in Makaz, Shaalbim, Beth-shemesh, and Elon-beth-hanan;
4.10: Ben-hesed, in Arubboth, with Socoh and all the land of Hepher;
4.11: Ben-abinadab, in all Naphath-dor—he had Taphath, Solomon's daughter, as his wife;
4.12: Baana son of Ahilud, in Taanach and Megiddo and all Beth-shean by Zarethan below Jezreel, from Beth-shean to Abel-meholah, beyond Jokneam;
4.13: Ben-geber, in Ramoth-gilead, with the towns of Jair son of Manasseh in Gilead and the region of Argob in Bashan—sixty great walled cities with bronze bars;
4.14: Ahinadab son of Iddo, in Mahanaim;
4.15: Ahimaaz, in Naphtali—he too had married a daughter of Solomon, Basemath;
4.16: Baanah son of Hushai, in Asher and Bealoth;
4.17: Jehoshaphat son of Paruah, in Issachar;
4.18: Shimei son of Ela, in Benjamin;
4.19: Geber son of Uri, in the land of Gilead—the territory of Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan. One governor oversaw the whole land.
4.20: Judah and Israel were as countless as the sand on the seashore. They ate and drank and were glad.
4.21: Solomon's dominion stretched from the Euphrates to the land of the Philistines and the border of Egypt. All those kingdoms paid tribute and served Solomon all the days of his life.
4.22: Solomon's daily provisions amounted to thirty cors of fine flour and sixty cors of ordinary flour,
4.23: ten fattened oxen, twenty pasture-fed cattle, a hundred sheep—besides deer, gazelles, roebucks, and choice birds.
4.24: He ruled all the territory west of the Euphrates—from Tiphsah to Gaza, over every king in that region. He had peace on every side.
4.25: Throughout Solomon's reign, Judah and Israel lived in security—from Dan to Beersheba, each family under their own vine and fig tree, at peace.
4.26: Solomon had forty thousand horse stalls for his chariots and twelve thousand horsemen.
4.27: Those district governors supplied provisions for King Solomon and all who ate at his table, each for his assigned month. Nothing was ever lacking.
4.28: Barley and straw for the horses and chariot teams—each governor brought them to the required place according to his duty.
4.29: God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding beyond all measure—a breadth of mind as vast as the sand on the seashore.
4.30: Solomon's wisdom surpassed all the wisdom of the east and all the wisdom of Egypt.
4.31: He was wiser than any man who had lived—wiser than Ethan the Ezrahite, wiser than Heman, Chalcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol. His fame spread to all the surrounding nations.
4.32: He composed three thousand proverbs, and his songs numbered a thousand and five.
4.33: He spoke of every kind of plant—from the great cedar of Lebanon down to the hyssop growing from a crack in the wall. He spoke of animals, birds, creeping things, and fish.
4.34: People came from every nation to hear Solomon's wisdom—delegates sent by every king on earth who had heard of him.

5.1: Hiram king of Tyre sent his envoys to Solomon when he heard that Solomon had been anointed king in his father's place—for Hiram had always loved David.
5.2: Solomon sent this word to Hiram:
5.3: 'You know that my father David could not build a house for the name of the LORD his God—the wars on every side prevented it, until the LORD finally put his enemies under his feet.'
5.4: 'But now the LORD my God has given me rest on every side. There is no adversary, no disaster to fear.'
5.5: 'So I intend to build a house for the name of the LORD my God, as the LORD said to my father David: Your son, whom I will place on your throne, will build my house.'
5.6: 'So order your men to cut cedar from Lebanon for me. My servants will work with yours, and I will pay whatever wages you set—for you know that none of us cuts timber like the Sidonians.'
5.7: When Hiram heard Solomon's words, he was delighted and said: 'Blessed be the LORD today, who gave David a wise son to rule this great people!'
5.8: Hiram sent Solomon this reply: 'I have received your message. I will do everything you desire—cedar and cypress timber, all of it.'
5.9: 'My servants will bring the timber down from Lebanon to the sea. I will make them into rafts and float them down the coast to where you direct. There they will be unloaded for you. In return, provide food for my household.'
5.10: So Hiram supplied Solomon with all the cedar and cypress timber he needed.
5.11: Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand cors of wheat for his household and twenty thousand cors of pure olive oil—year after year.
5.12: The LORD gave Solomon wisdom, as he had promised. Peace held between Hiram and Solomon, and the two kings made a covenant together.
5.13: King Solomon levied forced labor from all Israel—thirty thousand men.
5.14: He sent them to Lebanon in monthly shifts of ten thousand—one month in Lebanon, two months home. Adoniram supervised the labor.
5.15: Solomon also had seventy thousand burden-bearers and eighty thousand stonecutters in the hills—
5.16: —plus three thousand three hundred foremen who oversaw the work and directed the workforce.
5.17: At the king's command they quarried large, costly stones—dressed stone for the foundation of the house.
5.18: Solomon's workmen and Hiram's workmen and the craftsmen of Gebal cut and prepared the timber and stones for building the house.

6.1: In the four hundred and eightieth year after Israel came out of Egypt—in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, in the second month, Ziv—he began to build the house of the LORD.
6.2: The house King Solomon built for the LORD was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high.
6.3: The porch in front of the main hall was twenty cubits long—the full width of the building—and ten cubits deep.
6.4: He made windows with recessed frames for the house.
6.5: He built a side annexe against the outer walls of the house—running around both the main hall and the inner sanctuary—and he made side chambers throughout.
6.6: The lowest level of the annexe was five cubits wide, the middle level six cubits, and the top level seven cubits. He stepped the outer wall outward at each level so that the supporting beams would not be embedded in the walls of the house itself.
6.7: The house was built from stone dressed at the quarry—so that no hammer or chisel or any iron tool was heard at the site during construction.
6.8: The entrance to the lowest level of the side structure was on the south side, with a stairway rising to the middle level and from the middle to the third.
6.9: He built the house and finished it, roofing it with cedar beams and planks.
6.10: He built the side annexe along the full length of the house—each level five cubits high—and fastened it to the house with cedar timbers.
6.11: Then the word of the LORD came to Solomon:
6.12: 'Concerning this house you are building—if you walk in my statutes and obey my ordinances and keep all my commandments, I will fulfill through you the promise I made to your father David.'
6.13: 'I will dwell in the midst of Israel and will never abandon my people.'
6.14: So Solomon built the house and finished it.
6.15: He lined the interior walls from floor to ceiling with cedar boards—the entire inside was wood. He laid the floor with cypress planks.
6.16: He partitioned off the rear twenty cubits of the house from floor to ceiling with cedar boards—this inner room was the Most Holy Place, the Holy of Holies.
6.17: The main hall in front of the inner sanctuary was forty cubits long.
6.18: The cedar covering the interior was carved with gourd shapes and open flowers. All was cedar—not a stone was visible anywhere inside.
6.19: He prepared the innermost room—the inner sanctuary—to hold the ark of the covenant of the LORD.
6.20: The inner sanctuary was a perfect cube—twenty cubits long, wide, and high. He overlaid it with pure gold, and overlaid the cedar altar as well.
6.21: Solomon overlaid the interior of the house with pure gold. He stretched gold chains across the front of the inner sanctuary and overlaid everything with gold.
6.22: He overlaid the entire house with gold until every part was finished. The whole altar that stood before the inner sanctuary he overlaid with gold.
6.23: In the inner sanctuary he made two cherubim of olivewood, each ten cubits tall.
6.24: Each cherub had wings of five cubits on each side—ten cubits from wingtip to wingtip.
6.25: The second cherub was the same—ten cubits. Both cherubim were identical in size and form.
6.26: Each cherub stood ten cubits tall.
6.27: He placed the cherubim in the inner sanctuary. Their wings were spread so that one wing of the first cherub touched one wall, one wing of the second touched the other wall, and their inner wings met each other in the center of the room.
6.28: He overlaid the cherubim with gold.
6.29: He carved the walls throughout—inner rooms and outer rooms alike—with figures of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers.
6.30: He overlaid the floors of both the inner and outer rooms with gold.
6.31: For the entrance to the inner sanctuary he made olivewood doors set in five-sided doorposts.
6.32: On both olivewood doors he carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, and overlaid them with gold—pressing the gold onto the carved cherubim and palm trees.
6.33: For the entrance to the main hall he made olivewood doorposts forming a square frame—
6.34: —and two cypress doors, each with two folding panels.
6.35: He carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers on them and overlaid all the carving with gold, applied evenly.
6.36: He built the inner courtyard with three courses of dressed stone and one course of cedar beams.
6.37: The foundation of the house of the LORD was laid in the fourth year, in the month of Ziv.
6.38: In the eleventh year, in the eighth month—Bul—the house was finished in every detail, exactly as planned. Seven years it took to build.

19.1: Ahab reported to Jezebel everything Elijah had done—including how he had put all the prophets to death with the sword.
19.2: Jezebel sent a messenger to Elijah: 'May the gods do so to me and more besides if by this time tomorrow your life is not like the life of one of those prophets.'
19.3: Elijah saw the threat and fled for his life. He came to Beersheba in Judah and left his servant there.
19.4: He went on alone into the wilderness—a full day's journey—and collapsed under a juniper tree. He asked God to let him die. 'Enough,' he said. 'LORD, take my life. I am no better than my fathers.'
19.5: He lay down and fell asleep under the juniper tree. Then an angel touched him and said: 'Get up and eat.'
19.6: He looked up and there beside him was bread baked on hot coals and a jar of water. He ate and drank, then lay back down.
19.7: The angel of the LORD came a second time, touched him, and said: 'Get up and eat—the journey ahead is too long for you.'
19.8: He got up, ate and drank, and on the strength of that food traveled forty days and forty nights to Horeb, the mountain of God—the very mountain where Moses had met the LORD.
19.9: He found a cave there and spent the night in it. Then the word of the LORD came to him: 'What are you doing here, Elijah?'
19.10: 'I have been consumed with zeal for the LORD, the God of hosts. The people of Israel have abandoned your covenant, demolished your altars, and put your prophets to death. I am the only one left—and now they are hunting me down.'
19.11: The LORD said: 'Go out and stand on the mountain before the LORD.' Then the LORD himself passed by. A great and powerful wind went ahead, tearing mountains apart and shattering rocks—but the LORD was not in the wind. After the wind, an earthquake—but the LORD was not in the earthquake.
19.12: After the earthquake, fire—but the LORD was not in the fire. And after the fire: a gentle whisper, a voice in utter stillness.
19.13: When Elijah heard it, he wrapped his face in his cloak—covering himself before the presence of God—and went out and stood at the entrance of the cave. The voice came: 'What are you doing here, Elijah?'
19.14: 'I have been consumed with zeal for the LORD, the God of hosts. Israel has abandoned your covenant, torn down your altars, and killed your prophets. I alone am left—and they want my life.' Elijah repeats the same words, unchanged. The vision has not broken through his despair.
19.15: The LORD said: 'Go back the way you came—to the wilderness of Damascus. When you arrive, anoint Hazael as king over Aram.'
19.16: 'Anoint Jehu son of Nimshi king over Israel, and anoint Elisha son of Shaphat of Abel-meholah as prophet in your place.'
19.17: 'Whoever escapes Hazael's sword, Jehu will cut down. Whoever escapes Jehu's sword, Elisha will finish.'
19.18: 'But I have kept for myself seven thousand in Israel—every knee that has not bent to Baal, every mouth that has not kissed him. You are not as alone as you think.'
19.19: Elijah left and found Elisha son of Shaphat plowing with twelve yoke of oxen. Elisha was driving the last yoke. Elijah passed by and threw his cloak over him—an unmistakable act of calling.
19.20: Elisha left the oxen and ran after Elijah. 'Let me kiss my father and mother goodbye,' he said, 'and then I will follow you.' Elijah said: 'Go back—what have I done to hold you back?'
19.21: Elisha turned back, slaughtered his oxen, and boiled the meat on a fire of the plowing gear—burning his livelihood as he cooked it. He fed the people, then arose and followed Elijah, serving him from that day forward.

20.1: Ben-hadad king of Aram mustered his whole army—thirty-two vassal kings, cavalry and chariots—and marched up to besiege Samaria.
20.2: He sent messengers into the city to Ahab king of Israel. Ben-hadad's message was this:
20.3: 'Your silver and gold are mine. Your wives and your best children are mine.'
20.4: Ahab king of Israel answered: 'As you say, my lord the king. I and everything I have are yours.'
20.5: The messengers came again: 'Ben-hadad says this: I sent word demanding your silver, gold, wives, and children—'
20.6: '—but tomorrow about this time I will send my men through your palace and your servants' houses to take whatever they want.'
20.7: Ahab summoned all the elders of the land and said: 'See how this man is looking for a fight. He demanded my wives, my children, my silver and gold—and I agreed to all of it.'
20.8: All the elders and all the people said: 'Do not listen. Do not give in.'
20.9: Ahab told Ben-hadad's messengers: 'Tell my lord the king: Your first demands I will meet—but this further demand I cannot fulfill.' The messengers went and brought the reply.
20.10: Ben-hadad sent back his answer: 'May the gods deal with me in the harshest terms if there is enough dust in Samaria to give every one of my soldiers a fistful.'
20.11: Ahab sent back: 'Tell him—a man strapping on his armor should not boast like a man who has taken it off after the fight.'
20.12: Ben-hadad was drinking with the allied kings in the field encampments when this reply arrived. He said: 'Take your positions.' They moved into battle formation against the city.
20.13: Then a prophet approached Ahab king of Israel and said: 'The LORD says this: Do you see this vast army? I am handing it over to you today—and you will know that I am the LORD.'
20.14: 'Through whom?' Ahab asked. 'Through the young officers of the district governors,' the prophet said. 'Who will strike first?' 'You will,' he answered.
20.15: Ahab counted the young officers of the district governors: two hundred and thirty-two men. Then he counted all the fighting men of Israel: seven thousand.
20.16: They marched out at midday. Ben-hadad was drinking himself drunk in the field encampments—he and the thirty-two allied kings with him.
20.17: The young officers led the way out of the city. Ben-hadad's scouts reported: 'Men are coming out of Samaria.'
20.18: He said: 'Whether they come in peace or for battle—take them alive.'
20.19: The young officers came out of the city first, with the main army following behind them.
20.20: Each Israelite cut down his man. The Arameans broke and fled; Israel gave chase. Ben-hadad king of Aram escaped on horseback with a cavalry escort.
20.21: Ahab king of Israel went out and seized the horses and chariots. The Arameans suffered a devastating defeat.
20.22: The prophet came to Ahab and said: 'Strengthen yourself and take careful stock of your situation—because at the spring campaign season, the king of Aram will come against you again.'
20.23: Ben-hadad's officers advised him: 'Their gods are gods of the hills—that's why they beat us. Fight them on level ground and we will surely overpower them.'
20.24: 'Also do this: dismiss the vassal kings from their posts and put trained military commanders in their place.'
20.25: 'Rebuild your army to match what you lost—horse for horse, chariot for chariot. Fight them on the plain, and victory is certain.' He took their advice and acted on it.
20.26: When spring came, Ben-hadad mustered the Aramean forces and marched up to Aphek to fight against Israel.
20.27: Israel was also mustered and supplied and marched out to meet them. The Israelites camped across from the Arameans like two small flocks of goats—while the Arameans spread across the whole countryside.
20.28: A man of God came to Ahab king of Israel and said: 'The LORD says this: Because the Arameans have claimed that the LORD is only a god of the hills and not of the valleys—I will hand all this vast army over to you. And you will know that I am the LORD.'
20.29: The two armies faced each other for seven days. On the seventh day the battle erupted. Israel cut down a hundred thousand Aramean foot soldiers in a single day.
20.30: The survivors fled into Aphek, and the city wall collapsed on twenty-seven thousand more. Ben-hadad himself fled into the city and hid in an inner room.
20.31: His servants said to him: 'We have heard that the kings of Israel are men of covenant mercy. Let us put on sackcloth and ropes and go out to the king of Israel. Perhaps he will let you live.'
20.32: They put on sackcloth and ropes and came to Ahab king of Israel. 'Your servant Ben-hadad says: Please let me live.' Ahab replied: 'Is he still alive? He is my brother.'
20.33: The men seized on the word—they were watching for any encouragement—and said: 'Yes, your brother Ben-hadad.' 'Go, bring him,' said Ahab. Ben-hadad came out, and Ahab brought him up into the chariot.
20.34: Ben-hadad said: 'The cities my father seized from your father I will restore. And you may establish your own trading district in Damascus, just as my father had in Samaria.' Ahab said: 'I release you on these terms.' He made a covenant with Ben-hadad and sent him away.
20.35: One of the sons of the prophets, acting on the LORD's command, said to his companion: 'Strike me.' But the man refused.
20.36: The prophet said: 'Because you did not obey the word of the LORD, a lion will kill you when you leave me.' As soon as the man went away, a lion came and killed him.
20.37: He found another man and said: 'Strike me.' That man struck him and drew blood.
20.38: The prophet went and waited along the road for the king to pass, disguising himself with a cloth bandaged over his eyes.
20.39: As the king passed by, the prophet called out: 'Your servant was in the thick of the battle when a soldier brought a captive to me and said: Guard this man. If he escapes, your life is forfeit—or you pay a talent of silver.'
20.40: 'But while I was occupied with this and that, he was gone.' Ahab said: 'You have judged your own case—that is your sentence.'
20.41: He quickly pulled the bandage from his eyes. Ahab recognized him as one of the prophets.
20.42: He said: 'The LORD says this: Because you have let go the man I had consecrated to destruction, your life will pay for his and your people for his people.'
20.43: Ahab king of Israel went home sullen and resentful, and returned to Samaria.

21.1: After these events, there was a man named Naboth the Jezreelite whose vineyard lay right beside the palace of Ahab king of Samaria in Jezreel.
21.2: Ahab spoke to Naboth: 'Let me have your vineyard for a kitchen garden—it is right next to my house. I will give you a better vineyard in exchange, or if you prefer, I will pay you its fair price in silver.'
21.3: But Naboth said to Ahab: 'The LORD forbid that I should give you the covenant land of my fathers. This ground is not mine to sell.'
21.4: Ahab went home sullen and resentful over Naboth's answer: 'I will not give you my ancestral inheritance.' He lay down on his bed, turned his face away, and refused to eat.
21.5: Jezebel his wife came to him and said: 'Why are you so downcast? Why won't you eat?'
21.6: He told her: 'I asked Naboth the Jezreelite to give me his vineyard—either for payment or in exchange for another. He said: I will not give you my vineyard.'
21.7: Jezebel said: 'Is this how you govern Israel? Get up and eat—cheer up. I will get you the vineyard of Naboth the Jezreelite.'
21.8: She wrote letters in Ahab's name, sealed them with his royal seal, and sent them to the elders and leading men of Naboth's city.
21.9: The letters said: 'Call a public fast. Seat Naboth in a place of honor before the people.'
21.10: 'Then seat two men of no account across from him. Have them testify: He cursed God and the king. Then take him out and stone him to death.'
21.11: The men of the city—the elders and leading citizens—carried out Jezebel's orders to the letter.
21.12: They called a public fast and gave Naboth the seat of honor.
21.13: Two men of no account came in and sat across from Naboth. In front of all the people they gave their false testimony: 'Naboth cursed God and the king.' They dragged him outside the city and stoned him to death.
21.14: Word was sent back to Jezebel: 'Naboth has been stoned. He is dead.'
21.15: The moment Jezebel heard that Naboth was stoned and dead, she said to Ahab: 'Get up and take possession of the vineyard Naboth refused to sell you. He is dead. The vineyard is yours.'
21.16: The moment Ahab heard Naboth was dead, he got up and went down to take possession of the vineyard.
21.17: Then the word of the LORD came to Elijah the Tishbite:
21.18: 'Go down to meet Ahab king of Israel who is in Samaria. He is in Naboth's vineyard—he has gone down to seize it.'
21.19: 'Say to him: The LORD says this: You have murdered and now you seize. And say: The LORD says this: In the very place where dogs lapped up Naboth's blood, dogs will lap up your blood—your blood, Ahab.'
21.20: Ahab said to Elijah: 'So you have found me—my enemy?' Elijah answered: 'I have found you. Because you have sold yourself like a slave to do what is evil in the eyes of the LORD.'
21.21: 'I am bringing disaster on you. I will consume you utterly—I will cut off from Ahab every male, slave or free, in all of Israel.'
21.22: 'I will make your dynasty like Jeroboam son of Nebat's—like Baasha son of Ahijah's—because you have provoked my anger and led Israel into sin.'
21.23: 'And of Jezebel the LORD has spoken: The dogs will devour Jezebel in the field of Jezreel.'
21.24: 'Those of Ahab's house who die in the city—dogs will devour them. Those who die in the countryside—the birds of the sky will consume them.'
21.25: (No one sold himself to do evil in the LORD's sight like Ahab—and Jezebel his wife was the one who drove him to it.)
21.26: He was utterly detestable in his idol worship, following the Amorites' path—the very people the LORD had driven out before Israel.
21.27: When Ahab heard these words, he tore his clothes. He put on sackcloth directly against his skin, fasted, and walked around in mourning—genuinely shaken.
21.28: Then the word of the LORD came to Elijah the Tishbite:
21.29: 'Do you see how Ahab has humbled himself before me? Because he has done this—because he has genuinely humbled himself—I will not bring the disaster in his days. In his son's days I will bring it on his house.'

13.1: By the word of the LORD, a man of God came from Judah to Bethel. Jeroboam was standing at the altar about to burn incense.
13.2: He called out against the altar by the word of the LORD: 'Altar! Altar! This is what the LORD says: A son will be born to the house of David—his name will be Josiah—and he will sacrifice on you the very priests of the high places who burn incense here, and human bones will be burned on you.' (This prophecy would be fulfilled some three hundred years later, in the reign of Josiah of Judah.)
13.3: He gave a sign that very day: 'This is the sign the LORD has declared: the altar will split apart and the ashes on it will spill to the ground.'
13.4: When King Jeroboam heard what the man of God cried out against the altar at Bethel, he thrust out his hand from the altar and shouted, 'Seize him!' But his hand shriveled up and he could not pull it back.
13.5: The altar split apart and the ashes spilled from it, just as the man of God had declared by the word of the LORD.
13.6: The king said to the man of God: 'Please, entreat the LORD your God and pray for me, that my hand might be restored.' The man of God prayed to the LORD, and the king's hand was restored—just as it had been.
13.7: The king said to the man of God: 'Come home with me, eat something, and I will give you a reward.'
13.8: The man of God answered the king: 'Even if you gave me half your palace, I would not go with you, nor eat bread nor drink water here.'
13.9: 'The LORD commanded me by his word: Do not eat bread, do not drink water, and do not go back by the way you came.'
13.10: So he left by a different road and did not return the way he had come to Bethel.
13.11: An old prophet was living in Bethel. His sons came home and told him everything the man of God had done that day in Bethel—every word he had spoken to the king.
13.12: Their father asked: 'Which way did he go?' His sons pointed out the road the man of God from Judah had taken.
13.13: He said: 'Saddle the donkey.' They saddled it, and he rode away.
13.14: He rode after the man of God and found him sitting under an oak tree. 'Are you the man of God who came from Judah?' he asked. 'I am,' he said.
13.15: The old prophet said: 'Come home with me and eat.'
13.16: The man of God answered: 'I cannot return with you or go in with you. I will not eat bread or drink water with you in this place.'
13.17: 'The LORD's word to me was this: Do not eat bread there, do not drink water there, and do not go back the way you came.'
13.18: The old prophet said: 'I am also a prophet like you, and an angel told me by the word of the LORD: Bring him back home so he can eat and drink.' But he was lying.
13.19: So the man of God turned back with him and ate and drank at his house.
13.20: While they were sitting at the table, the word of the LORD came to the prophet who had brought him back.
13.21: He cried out to the man of God from Judah: 'This is what the LORD says: Because you defied the word of the LORD and did not keep the command the LORD your God gave you—'
13.22: 'because you came back and ate bread and drank water in the very place where he said, Do not eat, do not drink—your body will not be buried in the tomb of your fathers.'
13.23: After he had eaten and drunk, the old prophet saddled the donkey for the man of God he had brought back.
13.24: On the road a lion met him and killed him. His body lay on the road; the donkey stood beside it, and the lion stood beside the body.
13.25: Passersby saw the body on the road and the lion standing beside it. They went and reported it in the city where the old prophet lived.
13.26: When the old prophet who had brought him back heard it, he said: 'It is the man of God who defied the word of the LORD. The LORD gave him to the lion, and the lion tore him and killed him—exactly as the LORD had warned him.'
13.27: He said to his sons: 'Saddle the donkey.' They saddled it.
13.28: He went and found the body lying on the road, with the donkey and the lion still standing beside it. The lion had not eaten the body, nor had it touched the donkey.
13.29: The old prophet lifted the body of the man of God, laid it on the donkey, and brought it back to the city to mourn over him and bury him.
13.30: He laid the body in his own tomb, and they mourned over him, crying, 'Alas, my brother!'
13.31: After the burial, the old prophet said to his sons: 'When I die, bury me in this same grave. Lay my bones beside the bones of the man of God.'
13.32: 'For the word he proclaimed by the LORD against the altar in Bethel and against all the hilltop shrines in the cities of Samaria will certainly come true.'
13.33: Even after all this, Jeroboam did not turn from his evil ways. He kept consecrating priests for the high places from all kinds of people. Anyone who wanted to be could become a priest of the high places.
13.34: This became the sin that would bring down the house of Jeroboam—the sin that would cut it off and wipe it from the face of the earth.

14.1: At that time Abijah, Jeroboam's son, became ill.
14.2: Jeroboam said to his wife: 'Get up and disguise yourself so you won't be recognized as my wife. Go to Shiloh—Ahijah the prophet is there, the one who told me I would be king over this people.'
14.3: 'Take ten loaves of bread, some cakes, and a jar of honey. He will tell you what will happen to the boy.'
14.4: Jeroboam's wife did as he said. She went to Shiloh and came to Ahijah's house. But Ahijah could not see—his eyes had gone dim with age.
14.5: But the LORD had said to Ahijah: 'The wife of Jeroboam is on her way to ask you about her son—he is sick. Tell her this and this.' When she arrived, she was in disguise.
14.6: When Ahijah heard her footsteps at the door, he said: 'Come in, wife of Jeroboam. Why are you pretending to be someone else? I have been sent to you with a hard message.'
14.7: 'Go, tell Jeroboam: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I lifted you up from among the people and made you ruler over my people Israel.'
14.8: 'I tore the kingdom from the house of David and gave it to you. But you have not been like my servant David, who kept my commandments and followed me with his whole heart, doing only what was right in my sight.'
14.9: 'Instead you have done more evil than anyone before you. You made other gods and cast metal images to provoke me, and you have thrown me aside as if I did not exist.'
14.10: 'Therefore I am bringing disaster on the house of Jeroboam. I will cut off every male, slave or free, in Israel. I will sweep away the house of Jeroboam like dung that is swept away until nothing remains.'
14.11: 'Anyone of Jeroboam's house who dies in the city, the dogs will eat. Anyone who dies in the open field, the birds of the sky will eat. The LORD has spoken.'
14.12: 'Go home now. The moment your feet cross the city threshold, the child will die.'
14.13: 'All Israel will mourn for him and give him a proper burial. He is the only one in Jeroboam's house whom the LORD the God of Israel has found to have any goodness in him.'
14.14: 'The LORD will raise up for himself a king over Israel who will destroy the house of Jeroboam. And that day is coming—it has already begun.'
14.15: 'The LORD will strike Israel—shaking her like a reed in the water. He will uproot Israel from this good land he gave their fathers and scatter them beyond the Euphrates River, because they have provoked him to anger by making their Asherah poles.'
14.16: 'He will hand Israel over—because of the sins of Jeroboam, who sinned and who led all Israel into sin.'
14.17: Jeroboam's wife got up and left. She came to Tirzah, and the moment she stepped across the threshold, the child died.
14.18: They buried him, and all Israel mourned—just as the LORD had spoken through his servant Ahijah the prophet.
14.19: As for the rest of Jeroboam's acts—his wars and his reign—they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
14.20: Jeroboam reigned twenty-two years. He rested with his fathers, and his son Nadab succeeded him.
14.21: Rehoboam son of Solomon reigned in Judah. He was forty-one years old when he came to the throne and reigned seventeen years in Jerusalem—the city the LORD had chosen from all the tribes of Israel to place his name there. His mother was Naamah, an Ammonite.
14.22: Judah did what was evil in the sight of the LORD. Their sins provoked him to jealousy more than anything their ancestors had done.
14.23: They built hilltop shrines, standing-stones, and Asherah poles on every high hill and under every leafy tree.
14.24: There were also male shrine prostitutes in the land. Judah committed all the abominations of the nations the LORD had driven out before Israel.
14.25: In the fifth year of King Rehoboam, Shishak king of Egypt attacked Jerusalem.
14.26: He stripped the temple treasury and the royal palace treasury. He took everything—including all the gold shields Solomon had made.
14.27: In their place Rehoboam made bronze shields and entrusted them to the commanders of the guard who stood watch at the palace entrance.
14.28: Whenever the king went to the house of the LORD, the guard carried the shields out—and brought them back to the guardroom afterward.
14.29: As for the rest of Rehoboam's acts and all that he did, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah.
14.30: War continued between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout their lifetimes.
14.31: Rehoboam rested with his fathers and was buried in the city of David. His mother was Naamah, an Ammonite. His son Abijam succeeded him.

15.1: In the eighteenth year of King Jeroboam son of Nebat, Abijam became king of Judah.
15.2: He reigned three years in Jerusalem. His mother was Maacah daughter of Abishalom.
15.3: He walked in all the sins his father had committed before him. His heart was not fully devoted to the LORD his God, as the heart of David had been.
15.4: Nevertheless, for David's sake the LORD his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem—he kept a successor on the throne and preserved Jerusalem.
15.5: David had done what was right in the LORD's eyes and had not turned aside from any of his commands all his life—except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite.
15.6: War between Rehoboam's line and Jeroboam continued throughout Abijam's reign.
15.7: As for the rest of Abijam's acts and all he did, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah. There was war between Abijam and Jeroboam.
15.8: Abijam rested with his fathers and was buried in the city of David. His son Asa succeeded him.
15.9: In the twentieth year of Jeroboam king of Israel, Asa became king of Judah.
15.10: He reigned forty-one years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Maacah daughter of Abishalom.
15.11: Asa did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his ancestor David had done.
15.12: He expelled the male shrine prostitutes from the land and cleared out all the idols his predecessors had made.
15.13: He even removed his mother Maacah from being queen mother because she had made an obscene image for Asherah. Asa cut down the image and burned it in the Kidron Valley.
15.14: The hilltop shrines were not removed—yet Asa's heart was fully devoted to the LORD all his days.
15.15: He brought into the house of the LORD the sacred gifts his father had dedicated and the gifts he himself had dedicated—silver and gold and vessels.
15.16: War between Asa and Baasha king of Israel continued throughout their lifetimes.
15.17: Baasha king of Israel marched against Judah and fortified Ramah, cutting off all movement in or out of Asa's territory.
15.18: Asa took all the silver and gold still in the temple treasury and the palace treasury and handed it to his servants. He sent it to Ben-hadad son of Tabrimmon, son of Hezion, king of Syria in Damascus, with this message:
15.19: 'There is a treaty between me and you, just as there was between my father and your father. I am sending you a gift of silver and gold. Break your treaty with Baasha king of Israel so he will pull back from me.'
15.20: Ben-hadad agreed with King Asa and sent his army commanders against the cities of Israel. They struck Ijon, Dan, Abel-beth-maacah, all Chinneroth, and all the territory of Naphtali.
15.21: When Baasha heard this, he stopped building Ramah and withdrew to Tirzah.
15.22: Then King Asa issued a levy on all Judah—no exceptions—and they carried away the stones and timber Baasha had been using to build Ramah. With these materials Asa built Geba of Benjamin and Mizpah.
15.23: As for all the rest of Asa's acts—his strength, all he did, the cities he built—they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah. But in his old age his feet became diseased.
15.24: Asa rested with his fathers and was buried in the city of his ancestor David. His son Jehoshaphat succeeded him.
15.25: Nadab son of Jeroboam became king of Israel in the second year of Asa king of Judah. He reigned over Israel two years.
15.26: He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD. He walked in the footsteps of his father and in the sin by which Jeroboam had led Israel into sin.
15.27: Baasha son of Ahijah, from the tribe of Issachar, conspired against Nadab and struck him down at Gibbethon, which belonged to the Philistines. Nadab and all Israel had been laying siege to Gibbethon.
15.28: Baasha killed him in the third year of Asa king of Judah and took the throne.
15.29: The moment Baasha was king, he struck down the entire house of Jeroboam—he left not a single survivor. He destroyed them all, just as the LORD had spoken through his servant Ahijah the Shilonite.
15.30: This happened because of the sins Jeroboam had committed and led Israel into—the provocation by which he had angered the LORD the God of Israel.
15.31: As for the rest of Nadab's acts and all he did, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
15.32: War between Asa and Baasha king of Israel continued throughout their lifetimes.
15.33: In the third year of Asa king of Judah, Baasha son of Ahijah became king of all Israel at Tirzah. He reigned twenty-four years.
15.34: He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD. He walked in the way of Jeroboam and in the sin by which Jeroboam had led Israel into sin.

16.1: The word of the LORD came to Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha:
16.2: 'I lifted you from the dust and made you ruler over my people Israel. Yet you walked in the way of Jeroboam and led my people Israel into sin, provoking me to anger with their sins.'
16.3: 'I am about to sweep away Baasha and his entire house. I will make your dynasty like the house of Jeroboam son of Nebat.'
16.4: 'Anyone of Baasha's house who dies in the city, the dogs will eat. Anyone who dies in the open field, the birds of the sky will eat.'
16.5: As for the rest of Baasha's acts and all he did and his military might, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
16.6: Baasha rested with his fathers and was buried at Tirzah. His son Elah succeeded him.
16.7: The word of the LORD also came through Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha and his house, because of all the evil Baasha had done in the LORD's sight, provoking him to anger with everything he did—making himself like the house of Jeroboam—and also because he had struck down that dynasty.
16.8: In the twenty-sixth year of Asa king of Judah, Elah son of Baasha became king of Israel at Tirzah. He reigned two years.
16.9: His servant Zimri—commander of half his chariot forces—conspired against him. While Elah was at Tirzah drinking himself drunk in the house of Arza, the palace steward at Tirzah,
16.10: Zimri came in and struck him down and killed him. It was the twenty-seventh year of Asa king of Judah. Then Zimri took the throne.
16.11: The moment he sat on the throne, he struck down the entire house of Baasha—not a single male survived, whether kinsman or friend.
16.12: Zimri wiped out the entire house of Baasha, just as the LORD had spoken against Baasha through Jehu the prophet—
16.13: because of all the sins of Baasha and his son Elah—the sins they committed and led Israel into, provoking the LORD the God of Israel to anger with their empty idols.
16.14: As for the rest of Elah's acts and all he did, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
16.15: In the twenty-seventh year of Asa king of Judah, Zimri reigned seven days at Tirzah—the shortest reign in Israel's history. The army was encamped against Gibbethon, a Philistine town,
16.16: when word reached the camp that Zimri had conspired and killed the king. Right there in the camp, all Israel made Omri—the army commander—king over Israel.
16.17: Omri marched up from Gibbethon with all Israel and laid siege to Tirzah.
16.18: When Zimri saw that the city was taken, he went into the inner stronghold of the palace and burned it down over himself. He died,
16.19: because of the sins he had committed—doing what was evil in the LORD's sight, walking in the way of Jeroboam and in the sin by which Jeroboam had led Israel into sin.
16.20: As for the rest of Zimri's acts and the conspiracy he carried out, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
16.21: Then the people of Israel split into two camps. Half followed Tibni son of Ginath to make him king; the other half followed Omri.
16.22: The people behind Omri proved stronger than those behind Tibni son of Ginath. Tibni died, and Omri became king.
16.23: In the thirty-first year of Asa king of Judah, Omri became king of Israel and reigned twelve years—the first six at Tirzah.
16.24: He bought the hill of Samaria from a man named Shemer for two talents of silver and built a city on it. He named it Samaria after Shemer, the former owner.
16.25: Omri did what was evil in the sight of the LORD—worse than all who had come before him.
16.26: He walked completely in the way of Jeroboam son of Nebat, in the sins by which Jeroboam had led Israel into sin—provoking the LORD the God of Israel to anger with their empty idols.
16.27: As for the rest of Omri's acts and the power he displayed, they are recorded in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
16.28: Omri rested with his fathers and was buried in Samaria. His son Ahab succeeded him.
16.29: Ahab son of Omri became king of Israel in the thirty-eighth year of Asa king of Judah. He reigned twenty-two years.
16.30: Ahab son of Omri did what was evil in the sight of the LORD—more than all who had come before him.
16.31: As if walking in the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat were a small thing, he married Jezebel daughter of Ethbaal king of the Sidonians—and went to serve Baal and bow down to him.
16.32: He set up an altar for Baal in the temple of Baal that he built in Samaria.
16.33: Ahab also made an Asherah pole. Ahab did more to provoke the LORD the God of Israel to anger than any king of Israel before him.
16.34: During Ahab's reign, Hiel the Bethelite rebuilt Jericho. He laid its foundations at the cost of his firstborn son Abiram, and he set up its gates at the cost of his youngest son Segub—fulfilling to the letter the word the LORD had spoken through Joshua son of Nun centuries before.

17.1: Then Elijah the Tishbite, from the settlers of Gilead—whose very name means 'My God is Yahweh'—declared to Ahab: 'As the LORD the God of Israel lives—the one before whom I stand—there will be no dew or rain in the coming years except at my word.'
17.2: The word of the LORD came to Elijah:
17.3: 'Leave here. Turn east and hide yourself by the brook Cherith, east of the Jordan.'
17.4: 'You will drink from the brook. I have commanded the ravens to feed you there.'
17.5: He went and did exactly as the LORD commanded—he went and settled by the brook Cherith east of the Jordan.
17.6: The ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning, and bread and meat in the evening, and he drank from the brook.
17.7: After a while the brook dried up—there had been no rain in the land.
17.8: The word of the LORD came to him again:
17.9: 'Go now to Zarephath, which belongs to Sidon, and stay there. I have commanded a widow there to provide for you.'
17.10: He got up and went to Zarephath. When he arrived at the city gate, a widow was there gathering sticks. He called to her: 'Please bring me a little water in a vessel so I can drink.'
17.11: As she was going to get it, he called after her: 'Please bring me a piece of bread too.'
17.12: She said: 'As the LORD your God lives, I have nothing baked. I have only a handful of flour in a jar and a little oil in a jug. I am gathering a few sticks to go home and cook one last meal for my son and me—and then we will die.'
17.13: Elijah said: 'Do not be afraid. Go and do as you said—but first make a small cake for me and bring it out. Then make something for yourself and your son.'
17.14: 'For this is what the LORD the God of Israel says: The jar of flour will not be exhausted and the jug of oil will not run dry until the day the LORD sends rain on the land.'
17.15: She went and did exactly as Elijah said—and she and Elijah and her household ate for many days.
17.16: The jar of flour was never exhausted and the jug of oil never ran dry—just as the LORD had spoken through Elijah.
17.17: After these things, the son of the woman—the mistress of the house—became ill. His illness grew so severe that he stopped breathing.
17.18: She said to Elijah: 'What do you have against me, man of God? Did you come here to bring my sin to remembrance and kill my son?'
17.19: He said: 'Give me your son.' He took the boy from her arms, carried him up to the room where he was staying, and laid him on his own bed.
17.20: He cried out to the LORD: 'O LORD my God, have you brought disaster even upon this widow I am staying with, by killing her son?'
17.21: He stretched himself over the boy three times and cried out to the LORD: 'O LORD my God, let this child's breath of life come into him again!'
17.22: The LORD heard Elijah's voice. The breath of life returned to the boy, and he came back to life.
17.23: Elijah took the child and brought him down from the upper room and handed him to his mother. 'Look,' said Elijah, 'your son is alive.'
17.24: The woman said to Elijah: 'Now I know for certain that you are a man of God—and that the word of the LORD on your lips is truth.'

18.1: After a long time—in the third year—the word of the LORD came to Elijah: 'Go and present yourself to Ahab. I will send rain on the land.'
18.2: Elijah went to meet Ahab. The famine was severe in Samaria.
18.3: Ahab summoned Obadiah—whose name means 'servant of Yahweh'—who was in charge of the palace. Obadiah was a man who deeply feared the LORD.
18.4: When Jezebel was killing the LORD's prophets, Obadiah had taken a hundred of them and hidden them in two caves—fifty to a cave—and kept them alive with bread and water.
18.5: Ahab said to Obadiah: 'Let us go through the land to every spring and every valley. Perhaps we can find enough grass to keep the horses and mules alive and spare our animals.'
18.6: They divided the land between them. Ahab went one way, and Obadiah went another.
18.7: As Obadiah was on the road, Elijah met him. Obadiah recognized him and fell face-down before him. 'Is it really you, my lord Elijah?' he asked.
18.8: 'It is I,' Elijah said. 'Go tell your master: Elijah is here.'
18.9: Obadiah said: 'What have I done to deserve this? Why would you hand your servant over to Ahab to be killed?'
18.10: 'As the LORD your God lives, Ahab has sent to every nation and kingdom looking for you. And when they said, He is not here—he made each kingdom and nation swear an oath that they could not find you.'
18.11: 'And now you tell me to go say to my master: Elijah is here.'
18.12: 'The moment I leave you, the Spirit of the LORD will carry you away to who knows where. I will go tell Ahab, he will not find you, and he will kill me—though I have feared the LORD from my youth.'
18.13: 'Has no one told my lord what I did when Jezebel was killing the LORD's prophets? I hid a hundred of them in two caves, fifty to a cave, and kept them alive with bread and water.'
18.14: 'And now you tell me to go say: Elijah is here. He will kill me.'
18.15: Elijah answered: 'As the LORD of hosts lives—before whom I stand—I will surely present myself to Ahab today.'
18.16: So Obadiah went and told Ahab, and Ahab went to meet Elijah.
18.17: When Ahab saw Elijah, he said: 'Is that you—you troubler of Israel?'
18.18: He answered: 'I have not troubled Israel—you have. You and your father's house, because you abandoned the commandments of the LORD and went after the Baals.'
18.19: 'Now send word and assemble all Israel before me at Mount Carmel—and bring the four hundred and fifty prophets of Baal and the four hundred prophets of Asherah who eat at Jezebel's table.'
18.20: Ahab sent word throughout all Israel and gathered the prophets at Mount Carmel.
18.21: Elijah stepped before all the people and said: 'How long will you limp between two positions? If the LORD is God, follow him. If Baal, then follow him.' The people said nothing.
18.22: Elijah said to the people: 'I am the only prophet of the LORD remaining. Baal's prophets number four hundred and fifty.'
18.23: 'Bring two bulls. Let them choose one, cut it in pieces, and lay it on the wood—but put no fire to it. I will prepare the other bull, lay it on the wood, and put no fire to it either.'
18.24: 'You call on the name of your god, and I will call on the name of the LORD. The God who answers by fire—he is God.' All the people said: 'It is well spoken.'
18.25: Elijah said to the prophets of Baal: 'Choose your bull and prepare it first—you have the numbers. Call on the name of your god. But put no fire under it.'
18.26: They took the bull, prepared it, and called on the name of Baal from morning until noon. 'O Baal, answer us!' they cried. But there was no voice—no answer. They leaped around the altar they had made.
18.27: At noon Elijah mocked them: 'Shout louder! Surely he is a god! Maybe he is thinking, or busy with something, or off on a journey. Maybe he is asleep and needs to be woken up!'
18.28: They shouted louder and slashed themselves with swords and spears as was their practice, until the blood streamed down over them.
18.29: Midday passed, and they kept raving until the time of the evening sacrifice. But there was no voice, no answer, no one who took notice.
18.30: Then Elijah said to all the people: 'Come near to me.' They all came. He repaired the altar of the LORD that had been broken down.
18.31: Elijah took twelve stones—one for each tribe of the sons of Jacob, to whom the word of the LORD had come saying, 'Israel shall be your name.'
18.32: With these stones he built an altar in the name of the LORD and dug a trench around it large enough to hold about two seahs of seed.
18.33: He arranged the wood, cut the bull in pieces, and laid it on the wood. Then he said: 'Fill four large jars with water and pour them on the offering and the wood.'
18.34: 'Do it again,' he said. They did. 'Do it a third time,' he said. And they did.
18.35: Water ran down all around the altar and filled the trench.
18.36: At the time of the evening offering, Elijah came near and prayed: 'O LORD, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel—let it be known today that you are God in Israel, and that I am your servant, and that I have done all this at your word.'
18.37: 'Answer me, LORD! Answer me! Let this people know that you, the LORD, are God—and that you are the one turning their hearts back to you.'
18.38: The fire of the LORD fell and consumed the burnt offering, the wood, the stones, and the dust—and it licked up every drop of water in the trench.
18.39: When all the people saw it, they fell on their faces and cried: 'The LORD—he is God! The LORD—he is God!' (The very cry that echoes the meaning of Elijah's own name: My God is Yahweh.)
18.40: Elijah said to the people: 'Seize the prophets of Baal—do not let one escape.' They seized them, and Elijah brought them down to the brook Kishon and slaughtered them there.
18.41: Elijah said to Ahab: 'Go up, eat and drink—I can hear the sound of heavy rain coming.'
18.42: Ahab went up to eat and drink. Elijah climbed to the top of Carmel and crouched down on the ground, his face between his knees.
18.43: He said to his servant: 'Go up and look toward the sea.' The servant went and looked. 'There is nothing,' he said. 'Go again,' said Elijah. This happened seven times.
18.44: At the seventh time the servant said: 'A small cloud is rising from the sea—no bigger than a man's hand.' Elijah said: 'Go tell Ahab: Hitch your chariot and go down before the rain traps you.'
18.45: Within a short while the sky turned black with clouds and wind, and a heavy rain began to fall. Ahab mounted his chariot and rode for Jezreel.
18.46: The LORD's power came upon Elijah. He girded up his robe and ran ahead of Ahab's chariot all the way to the entrance of Jezreel.

7.1: Solomon took thirteen years to build his own palace—and finished every part of it.
7.2: He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon—a hundred cubits long, fifty wide, thirty high—supported by four rows of cedar pillars with cedar beams above.
7.3: A cedar ceiling covered the beams above forty-five pillars arranged fifteen per row.
7.4: Three rows of windows faced each other on three tiers, letting light fall symmetrically.
7.5: Every door and doorframe was square; the windows faced each other across three levels.
7.6: He also built the Hall of Pillars—fifty cubits long, thirty wide—with a columned porch across the front.
7.7: He built the Hall of the Throne—the Hall of Justice—where Solomon would pronounce judgment. Cedar covered it from floor to ceiling.
7.8: His personal residence—set in a second courtyard behind the Hall of Pillars—was built to the same design. He also built a matching palace for Pharaoh's daughter, whom he had taken in marriage.
7.9: All these buildings were of costly hewn stone—precisely measured, sawn smooth on every face—from foundation to roofline, extending outward to the great outer court.
7.10: The foundation blocks were enormous—costly hewn stones, some ten cubits long, some eight.
7.11: Above them came more precisely hewn stones of equal quality, and cedar.
7.12: The great outer court was bordered by three rows of hewn stone and one of cedar beams—the same as the inner court of the LORD's house and the temple porch.
7.13: King Solomon sent to Tyre and summoned Hiram.
7.14: He was the son of a Naphtalite widow; his father was a Tyrian bronze craftsman. Hiram himself was filled with wisdom, understanding, and skill in all bronze work. He came to King Solomon and executed everything Solomon required.
7.15: He cast two bronze pillars, each eighteen cubits high, with a circumference of twelve cubits.
7.16: He cast two bronze capitals to crown the tops of the pillars, each five cubits high.
7.17: He fashioned latticed checkerwork and chains of twisted cord for the capitals—seven rows per capital.
7.18: Two rows of pomegranates encircled the latticework covering each capital—the same arrangement for both pillars.
7.19: The capitals topping the porch pillars were shaped like lilies, four cubits high.
7.20: Above the rounded bulge beside each lattice, two hundred pomegranates encircled each capital in rows—on both pillars.
7.21: He erected the pillars at the temple entrance. The right pillar he named Jachin ('He establishes'); the left pillar he named Boaz ('In him is strength').
7.22: The tops were finished with lily-work. The pillars were done.
7.23: He cast the great Sea: a circular bronze basin ten cubits across, five cubits deep, thirty cubits around.
7.24: Below the rim, two rows of gourd-shaped knobs encircled the entire Sea—ten per cubit—cast as one piece with the basin.
7.25: The Sea rested on twelve bronze oxen—three facing north, three west, three south, three east—all with their hindquarters turned inward.
7.26: A handbreadth thick, its rim shaped like a lily-flower cup. It held two thousand baths.
7.27: He made ten bronze stands—each four cubits long, four wide, three high.
7.28: Each stand was built with panels fitted within frames.
7.29: On the panels were lions, oxen, and cherubim. Garlands of hammered metal ran above and below the figures.
7.30: Each stand rolled on four bronze wheels with bronze axles. Four corner supports held the basin, with garlands flanking every mounting.
7.31: A collar one cubit high rose above the top of each stand. The circular opening rested on a base a cubit and a half deep, surrounded by square carved panels.
7.32: Four wheels beneath the panels were joined by axles fixed into the stand. Each wheel was a cubit and a half in diameter.
7.33: The wheels were chariot-style: axles, hubs, spokes, and rims all cast in bronze.
7.34: Four corner supports—cast as one with the stand—stood at each corner.
7.35: A circular band half a cubit high crowned each stand, its projections and panels all one piece with it.
7.36: On every panel and border he engraved cherubim, lions, and palm trees—each proportioned to its space—with garlands encircling everything.
7.37: He made all ten stands the same way: one mold, one size, one form.
7.38: He made ten bronze basins, one per stand—each holding forty baths, four cubits across.
7.39: Five stands he positioned on the south side of the temple, five on the north. The Sea he placed at the southeast corner.
7.40: Hiram made the basins, shovels, and bowls as well. And with that, Hiram completed all the work he had done for King Solomon on the LORD's house:
7.41: the two pillars; the two bowl-shaped capitals crowning them; the two latticed coverings for the bowls;
7.42: four hundred pomegranates for the two lattices—two rows per lattice—covering the bowl-shaped capitals;
7.43: the ten stands and their ten basins;
7.44: the one Sea and the twelve oxen beneath it;
7.45: and the pots, shovels, and basins. Every vessel Hiram made for the LORD's house was of polished bronze.
7.46: The king had them all cast in the clay soil of the Jordan Valley between Succoth and Zarethan.
7.47: Solomon never weighed the bronze—there was so much of it that the total was never tallied.
7.48: Solomon also furnished the LORD's house: the gold altar, the gold table for the bread of the Presence,
7.49: ten lampstands of pure gold—five right, five left before the inner sanctuary—with flowers, lamps, and tongs, all gold;
7.50: cups, snuffers, basins, spoons, and fire pans of pure gold; and gold hinges for the doors of the Most Holy Place and for the doors of the main hall.
7.51: All the work King Solomon had done on the LORD's house was finished. He brought in the silver, gold, and vessels that his father David had dedicated, and stored them in the treasuries of the LORD's house.

8.1: Solomon assembled all the elders of Israel—the heads of the tribes and the leaders of every family—summoned to Jerusalem to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD from the city of David, which is Zion.
8.2: All Israel assembled to King Solomon at the great feast in the month of Ethanim—the seventh month.
8.3: When all the elders of Israel had gathered, the priests lifted the ark.
8.4: They brought up the ark of the LORD, the tent of meeting, and all the sacred vessels that had been kept there. The priests and Levites carried everything.
8.5: King Solomon and the entire assembly of Israel stood before the ark, offering sheep and oxen in numbers too great to count.
8.6: The priests carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD to its resting place: the Most Holy Place—beneath the spread wings of the cherubim.
8.7: The cherubim spread their wings over the ark's place, overshadowing both the ark and its poles.
8.8: The poles were long enough that their tips showed from the Holy Place just outside the inner sanctuary—but not from outside the building. They are still there to this day.
8.9: The ark contained only the two stone tablets Moses had placed there at Horeb—the covenant the LORD made with Israel when he brought them out of Egypt.
8.10: When the priests came out of the Holy Place, a cloud filled the house of the LORD—
8.11: so thick that the priests could not remain to serve. The glory of the LORD had filled his house—as it had once filled the tabernacle.
8.12: Then Solomon said: 'The LORD has declared that he would dwell in thick darkness.'
8.13: I have truly built you a magnificent house—a settled place for you to dwell in forever.
8.14: Then the king turned to face the whole assembly of Israel—all standing—and blessed them.
8.15: He said: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel! He spoke it to my father David with his own mouth—and has fulfilled it with his own hand:'
8.16: From the day I brought my people Israel out of Egypt, I chose no city among all Israel's tribes for a house where my name would dwell. But I chose David to lead my people Israel.
8.17: My father David set his heart on building a house for the name of the LORD, the God of Israel.
8.18: But the LORD said to my father: You did well to set your heart on building a house for my name.
8.19: Nevertheless, the building is not yours to do. Your son—your own flesh—he will build the house for my name.
8.20: The LORD has kept his word. I have succeeded my father David, I sit on Israel's throne as the LORD promised—and I have built the house for the name of the LORD, the God of Israel.
8.21: And I have prepared a place for the ark—the ark that holds the covenant the LORD made with our fathers when he brought them out of Egypt.
8.22: Solomon stood before the altar of the LORD, facing the whole assembly of Israel, and spread his hands toward heaven.
8.23: He said: 'LORD, God of Israel, there is no God like you—in heaven above or on earth beneath. You keep covenant and faithful love for your servants who walk before you with their whole heart.'
